feedback.text: Dear Members of the European Commission, The Digital Fairness Act is a promising foundation for consumer rights in the digital age. However, to deliver true fairness, it must go beyond transparency and encompass real ownership of digital goodsjust as with physical products. 1. Ownership Must Mean Permanence When a consumer buys a digital productbe it a video game, film, eBook, or piece of softwarethey should own it indefinitely, not just lease access under a license that can be revoked at any time. If someone purchases a DVD, they can watch it forever. The same should apply to a digitally purchased movie or game. Ownership implies the right to use, access, store, transfer, and preserve the contentregardless of server shutdowns or licensing changes. 2. Inheritance and Digital Second-Hand Markets Just like physical collections, digital media must be inheritable and resalable. Consumers should be legally allowed to sell or gift their digital libraries, whether games, books, or software, just as they would with physical goods. This would not only respect the consumers rights but also stimulate a healthy secondary market. A standardized, legally protected system for resale and transfer of digital licenses should be required across all platforms. 3. Live-Service Games Need End-of-Life Obligations Live-service games such as Anthem, Marvel Heroes, or The Crew (2014) have left paying customers with nothing after servers were shut down. This practice must be legally addressed. Publishers must be required to submit an "End-of-Life Plan" before releasedetailing how consumers can continue to access the product if online services are discontinued. Acceptable solutions could include: Release of server code for private hosting, Transition to offline or LAN-enabled modes, Transfer of community management rights under fair-use licensing. 4. Safeguards Against Retroactive Content Changes In recent years, companies have retroactively altered games or media post-purchaseremoving or modifying content due to political, social, or commercial reasons. Whether these changes involve violence, sexuality, language, or ideological themes, consumers should retain access to the original version they paid for. The right to archive, preserve, and access unaltered digital works must be protectedregardless of evolving corporate policies. 5. Preservation and Public Access for Abandoned Software When software or games become unavailabledue to bankruptcy, disinterest, or expired licensesthe public must have a right to preserve and re-access these works. A "use-it-or-free-it" clause could mandate that, after a defined period of unavailability (e.g., 35 years), copyright holders must either re-release the product or allow public hosting under limited licenses. This would prevent the loss of cultural heritage and the disappearance of entire player communities. 6. Fair Copyright Limitations for Digital Decay Unlike books or films, digital games often become unplayable once publisher servers go offline. In such cases, retaining full copyright creates a paradox: the rights exist, but the work is inaccessible. This undermines the very purpose of copyrightencouraging access and creation. Legislators should consider limited exceptions for digital content preservation when: No legal access is available, The original product is no longer supported, The community expresses cultural or historical interest. Conclusion If the EU aims for real fairness, digital goods must no longer be treated as temporary, revocable licenses. They must be treated as propertytransferable, preservable, and protected. Whether its a game, a film, or a digital tool, ownership must mean more than a login and a fragile license. The Digital Fairness Act has the potential to be groundbreakingbut only if it enshrines these principles of digital permanence, resale rights, consumer control, and cultural preservation. We urge you to seize this opportunity
